item.title: The AI build-out is powering global goods trade
item.description: Data centre boom is helping to mask the impact of Trump tariffs on US and world economy

item.title: Goldman reports best quarter in 5 years even as fixed-income traders disappoint
item.description: Equities traders powered bank’s earnings, while fixed-income, currencies and commodities business fell well short

item.title: Why time is on Iran’s side
item.description: A global energy crisis is only just beginning. Political turmoil will follow

item.title: Perella Weinberg to buy London advisory boutique Gleacher Shacklock
item.description: Acquisition is latest move by US investment banks to expand in Europe

item.title: Private credit has calmed the credit cycle
item.description: We’d miss it if it went

item.title: Viktor Orbán ousted after 16 years in landslide defeat for anti-EU torchbearer
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Brussels’ energy crisis remedies and Green Deal architect returns

item.title: Bill Ackman plays to the market
item.description: Plus, Nest’s private credit mandate, US small caps jump and Rothko in Florence

item.title: How the Federal Reserve could shrink its balance sheet
item.description: Suggestions for how the US central bank can lower its influence in financial markets are coming thick and fast

item.title: Five UK water companies spent more than £32mn on appeal to raise bills
item.description: Utilities argued for additional increases to attract investors and avoid breaching licence conditions

item.title: Weight-loss drugs and Mars bars: Novo Nordisk’s comeback bid
item.description: The maker of Wegovy and Ozempic wants to learn lessons from consumer groups to crack the US market

item.title: Pret A Manger owner JAB books $6bn in 2025 from JDE Peet’s sale
item.description: Wealth manager for billionaire Reimann family has overhauled its investment focus from consumer sector to insurers

item.title: Boss of state-controlled postal group defends €10bn bid for Telecom Italia
item.description: Matteo Del Fante: ‘We have demonstrated that we are a market company’

item.title: Luxury retailer Mytheresa has sights on Middle East growth despite conflict
item.description: New CEO wants to increase investment given concentration of wealthy potential clients in the Gulf

item.title: Carson Block sees new dawn for short sellers in AI disruption
item.description: Muddy Waters founder says bets against US stocks will have ‘wind at their backs’ as technology upends markets

item.title: UK state-backed fund Nest invests £450mn in US private credit
item.description: Workplace pension scheme targets 30% allocation to private markets by 2030 despite growing strains on asset class

item.title: How Strathberry put Scottish handbags on the luxury map
item.description: The label’s ‘high-end quality at accessible prices’ ethos is resonating with shoppers disillusioned with top luxury houses

item.title: Sotheby’s offers to pay sellers interest as art market struggles
item.description: Patrick Drahi-owned auction house contends with heavy debt burden in shrinking market

item.title: ‘He knows how to get his way’: Labour’s survivor, Ed Miliband
item.description: The UK energy secretary is a right-wing target, but supporters argue that his renewables push is not zealotry

item.title: Carmakers’ new weapon to tackle EV range anxiety
item.description: VW, Renault and BMW considering range-extended vehicles to entice drivers not ready for full electric switch

item.title: Meta builds AI version of Mark Zuckerberg to interact with staff
item.description: Chief executive is training and testing his own character as part of wider push to develop ‘personal superintelligence’

item.title: Hungary ousts longtime prime minister Viktor Orbán
item.description: Voters hand opposition leader Péter Magyar a landslide win in election with record turnout

item.title: Breaking cover: the future of the business book
item.description: Authors are experimenting with new technology and formats to better communicate ideas

item.title: The less technically literate might be a touch concerned that your AI escaped
item.description: A behind-the-scenes look at the work of Rutherford Hall, critical communications strategist

item.title: UK households’ living standards to fall after energy price shock
item.description: Report from Resolution Foundation urges ministers to offer only targeted support to poorer households

item.title: FirstFT: Viktor Orbán concedes defeat in Hungarian election
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: Trump announces naval blockade of Strait of Hormuz, and China open to Taiwanese TV and imports

item.title: Oil prices expected to rise as hope fades of end to Iran war
item.description: Crude had stabilised on optimism that a deal could be reached to reopen the Strait of Hormuz

item.title: Lidl stirs up mobile market with discount phone plans
item.description: Chain to expand beyond Germany, Austria and Switzerland with service that could be available in up to 30 countries

item.title: Could US pensioners be swallowed by the next credit wave?
item.description: Trump wants retirees’ money in private credit, but it’s a bad idea at this stage of the cycle

item.title: Cancer drug licensed by GSK from China posts promising trial results
item.description: Mo-Rez, developed by Hansoh Pharma, is a next-generation treatment for ovarian and endometrial cancer

item.title: Fees for seas: a history of taxing waterways
item.description: From Ottoman sultans to Danish kings, Iran’s toll system for Strait of Hormuz revives an old maritime fight
